ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for stir-fried mixed vegetables (carrot, broccoli, mushroom, capsicum); closest defensible match is the generic stir-fried vegetable dish class FOODON_03311737.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables and vegetable products — cooked vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (cooked vegetables) is the closest defensible category for a stir-fried mixed vegetable dish; no exact composite dish code exists in the public FoodEx2 hierarchy for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for stir-fried mixed vegetable dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried mixed vegetables as served in a Singaporean hawker centre or home meal is approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried mixed vegetables — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database, record F14080052
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for stir-fried mixed vegetables, record identifier F14080052. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Cholesterol (1.67 mg), saturated fat (1.0 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and vitamin C (20.0 mg) were originally tagged to a delivery-platform source in the input record; these four values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 11900, mixed vegetables cooked from frozen, and composite stir-fry vegetable entries) and are consistent with expected physiological ranges for a plant-based stir-fry with minimal animal-derived sauce. HPB is treated as the primary source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy serves as the secondary cross-check for these four fields only.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy mixed vegetables — analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database entries for constituent non-starchy vegetables including broccoli, mushroom, red capsicum, and carrot
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried mixed vegetables (carrot, broccoli, mushroom, red capsicum) as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database entries for the constituent non-starchy vegetables: broccoli (GI approximately 10–15), mushrooms (GI approximately 10), red capsicum (GI approximately 15), and carrot (GI approximately 35 raw, approximately 47 cooked but very low GL due to low carbohydrate density). The weighted composite GI for this low-carbohydrate dish (2.57 g carbohydrate per 100 g) is estimated at approximately 15 (low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (15 × 7.71 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.16, rounded to 1.2. This represents a very low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 268 mg per 100 g; moderate for a cooked vegetable dish but manageable with sauce reduction
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation to bring sodium below 200 mg per 100 g; the dish's potassium, fibre, and vitamin C content from broccoli and red capsicum provide meaningful cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.57 g per 100 g), low GI (approximately 15), and dietary fibre 2.17 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is highly suitable for type 2 diabetes management; the negligible available carbohydrate, low glycemic index, and fibre content from broccoli, carrot, and capsicum collectively minimise postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, and antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is cardioprotective due to low saturated fat, absent trans fat, and the presence of sulforaphane (broccoli), beta-carotene (carrot), and vitamin C (red capsicum, broccoli); sodium should be moderated by requesting less sauce to further reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients from broccoli, mushrooms, and carrots; nightshade content from red capsicum
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish provides strong anti-inflammatory benefit through sulforaphane, beta-glucans, and beta-carotene; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor response to red capsicum and confirm cooking oil is not palm-based.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Mixed Vegetables (炒杂菜, Sayur Campur Goreng) is a widely consumed Singaporean and Chinese vegetable dish consisting of carrot, broccoli, mushroom, and red capsicum stir-fried at high heat with aromatics such as garlic and ginger, typically seasoned with soy sauce and a small amount of oyster sauce. The dish is characterised by a lightly caramelised exterior on the vegetables, a tender-crisp texture, and a savoury umami flavour profile. It is a staple side dish in Singaporean hawker centres, Chinese restaurants, and home kitchens, commonly served alongside rice or noodles. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate, and low-GI dish providing 61 kcal per 100 g, 2.3 g protein, 4.6 g fat, and only 2.57 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is 2.17 g per 100 g. Sodium is 268 mg per 100 g, which can be reduced by requesting less sauce. The dish is rich in vitamin C (20 mg per 100 g) from broccoli and red capsicum, iron (1.53 mg per 100 g), and calcium (23 mg per 100 g). It is suitable for type 2 diabetes, hypertension (with sauce moderation), and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly. A standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing approximately 183 kcal and a glycemic load of only 1.2.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (record F14080052), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values were originally tagged to a delivery-platform source in the input; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite vegetable entries and are consistent with expected physiological ranges. HPB is treated as the primary source throughout. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database constituent vegetable entries, which is the approved GI source, with full estimation basis documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried vegetable dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F14080052, nor are they available for an equivalent composite dish in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. These fields are genuinely unavailable in both the primary approved source (HPB) and the secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) for this specific composite preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): HPB source reports 0 g trans fat for this dish, consistent with a plant-based stir-fry using standard cooking oil. cholesterol_mg (1.67 mg per 100 g) is a trace amount consistent with minimal animal-derived sauce ingredients; value cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input record contained duplicate portion entries (two identical 1 plate / 300 g entries from grabfood); deduplicated to a single serving entry. The input dietary_tags included keto; this has been removed from dietary_flags as the dish contains 2.57 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 7.71 g per 300 g serving, which is borderline for strict ketogenic thresholds and not a standard keto-certified classification. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database constituent vegetable entries; no direct composite dish GI measurement exists in the approved database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (15 × 7.71) / 100 = 1.16, rounded to 1.2.
